pr.title: Create a Getting Started guide for new contributors
pr.author: stephenjayakar

summary: Added onboarding documentation for new Melee decomp contributors, centered on a new GETTING_STARTED.md explaining the project goal of matching PowerPC assembly 100%, basic decomp.me scratch workflow, context/header usage, how to choose unclaimed assembly, and starter resources. README.md was reorganized to link the new guide, add contributing/FAQ material, and discuss compiler/register-allocation uncertainty and permuter use. CONTRIBUTING.md gained auto-formatting guidance with git clang-format and clearer PR/onboarding advice, including the option to submit a decomp.me issue instead of a full PR. Review discussion refined the docs around static context limitations, Trello/Discord access, avoiding unsupported legal claims, and making examples more suitable for beginners.
what_changed: Created GETTING_STARTED.md with an introduction to Melee decompilation, explaining that project work is to write C which compiles to a 100% match against PowerPC assembly.
Added an example C function, ftMewtwo_SpecialHi_CreateGFX, alongside corresponding PowerPC assembly to illustrate the C-to-ASM matching target.
Documented a decomp.me starter flow: create a new scratch, choose GameCube/Wii, use the Super Smash Bros. Melee preset, paste context, paste target assembly, and create the scratch.

Explained decomp.me-generated temporary/unknown code such as temp_r31 and unk2C, and how raw pointer-offset accesses can later be interpreted using project context, such as identifying arg0 as an HSD_GObj-related fighter object and using getFighter.

Added guidance for choosing starter work: find short unclaimed assembly, avoid already-decompiled labels, check the public Trello board, search the repo for existing definitions, and search decomp.me for existing scratches.
Added resources including a PowerPC ISA PDF and an external more detailed getting-started guide.

Updated README.md heading structure, added a Contributing section linking GETTING_STARTED.md and CONTRIBUTING.md, and added FAQ text about post-decompilation goals, compiler knowledge, register allocation, and source permutation.
Updated CONTRIBUTING.md with an Auto Formatting section recommending clang-format via git clang-format on staged C files.

Updated CONTRIBUTING.md Pull Requests section to tell beginners they can submit a decomp.me link through a GitHub issue instead of creating a full PR, and to group local changes into fewer larger PRs because review capacity is limited.
Added images/compilation_diagram.png as a visual aid referenced by the getting-started guide.
smart_moves: Lowered the barrier for first-time contributors by documenting the decomp.me path, including a no-local-build option where a contributor can open an issue with a decomp.me link and let maintainers integrate it.
Made the matching objective explicit: readability can be secondary initially, but the produced C must compile to the original PowerPC assembly 100%.
Used a concrete function and assembly pair to connect high-level C, decomp.me output, and low-level instructions rather than only describing the workflow abstractly.
Included practical triage advice for avoiding duplicated work: check Trello claims, repo definitions, and existing decomp.me scratches before starting.
Added formatting guidance directly to CONTRIBUTING.md so code style expectations are discoverable before review.

Responded to review by replacing or improving some wording: added git clang-format details, clarified Trello is read-only unless joining Discord, and avoided including an auto-invite Trello link in public docs.
decomp_lessons: For onboarding docs, explain that a matched C function normally replaces assembly removed from the repo; contributors need to understand the .s-to-.c lifecycle.

decomp.me context should be treated like a large header include, but reviewers noted that a static pasted context is often insufficient; future docs should explain generated context tooling such as gen.sh or gcc -E-based preprocessing once stable.
Raw decompiler output with temp registers and unk offsets can match, but contributors should use file location, known structs, and helper functions to recover meaningful types and field accesses.
When choosing a beginner task, avoid claimed files, already-implemented labels, and active decomp.me scratches to reduce duplicate work.
Compiler register allocation is uncertain without compiler source; small source changes, even variable names according to the README FAQ, can affect allocation, and source permuters may be needed.
Review capacity is a project constraint; documentation should encourage whole-file or grouped contributions where possible instead of many tiny PRs.
naming_conventions: CONTRIBUTING.md retained guidance to avoid naming a function before matching and understanding it.

For Melee-related functions, names should use the file prefix, lower camel casing for a multi-word function filename, and upper camel casing for the function name itself unless it is a standard library reimplementation.
Examples in the style guide include lbVector_sin, lbArchive_Parse, Player_DoThing, Stage_GetLeftBlastZone, and ftFox_LaserOnDeath.
Struct fields in larger Melee structs, such as fighter/player structs, should be prefixed with their offset, with examples like x0_thing and x4_filler.
If structs are copied from another project, remove members that are not part of the submitted code or existing code.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Use decomp.me with Platform set to GameCube / Wii and preset set to Super Smash Bros. Melee for scratch work.
Paste target assembly and context into decomp.me; the context acts like a large header file included by the scratch.
Start from decompiler output such as temp_r31 = arg0->unk2C, then translate unknown memory accesses into raw pointer-offset C if necessary to understand or match behavior.
Prefer improving raw offset code into typed code when evidence allows, e.g. using Fighter*, HSD_GObj*, getFighter, and known callback fields.
Use repository label searches to determine whether a function is already decompiled before starting.
Use decomp.me searches for the label to detect whether someone else has already attempted or is actively working on a scratch.
For difficult register allocation, the README FAQ points toward source code permutation with decomp-permuter as an option.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.feedback: Requested mention of git clang-format and possibly VS Code setup guidance for formatting.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.feedback: Suggested clearer fork/PR wording and emphasized matching whole files at a time because PR reviewers are scarce.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.feedback: Suggested mentioning an easier path similar to issue submission with a decomp.me link, rather than requiring forking, compiling locally, and creating a PR.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.feedback: Questioned whether the initial code sample was too unclean for documentation and suggested finding a prettier snippet; author responded that the example was short and showed matching priority over prettiness.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.feedback: Warned that a static context will not usually be sufficient and said context-generation tools need cleanup and usage instructions; suggested looking at gen.sh.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.feedback: Noted that the public Trello link is not an auto-invite unlike the Discord version, and recommended mentioning the difference rather than exposing the auto-invite link.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.feedback: Objected to making legal claims without citation.

follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that updated GETTING_STARTED.md context-generation instructions or documented gen.sh.
Find later PRs that filled in the README TODO for setting up the source permuter.
Find documentation changes around decomp.me issue submission workflow for beginner contributors.
Find PRs that revised CONTRIBUTING.md naming, struct-offset, or clang-format guidance.
Find PRs that replaced or expanded the getting-started code example used for decomp.me onboarding.
